top10.introHtml: Top 10 picks for a cheap, fun second car — ranked by condition over miles. All are verified convertibles in the $7k–$15k range within 35 miles of Media, PA (ZIP 19063), sourced from Cars.com, CarGurus, and AutoTrader as of today. A pre-purchase inspection (PPI) is strongly recommended on any of these.

picks.whyGood: The Solstice GXP packs a 260hp Ecotec 2.0T turbo — far more fun than the base Solstice's 177hp N/A four. At 51,730 miles and $11,794 (with a recent $500 price drop), this is rare: a turbo roadster with low-ish miles at a fair price. Manual transmission likely available on GXP. Pontiac built this on the Kappa platform shared with the Saturn Sky Red Line — reliable powertrain with strong aftermarket support.
picks.whatToVerify: Operate the top through full close/open cycle — check for tears, seam leaks at header rail, and water stains on carpet/headliner. Look for rocker-panel rust (Kappa cars can develop it). Turbo boost gauge on test drive (IHI turbo should spool smoothly). Check for leaking turbo coolant lines — common if car sat. Inspect hardtop latches if equipped.
picks.bottomLine: Best power-per-dollar convertible in this search — the GXP turbo at 51k miles is a rare find under $12k.

picks.whyGood: At $7,500 with a Great Deal badge and 62,995 miles, this is one of the best-value fun cars in the search. The base Solstice (2.4L Ecotec, 177hp) is light, rear-wheel-drive, and has a tight suspension. Kappa platform is known for durability. Great entry point — if you want more power, the GXP above is worth the extra $4k.
picks.whatToVerify: Same top inspection as GXP: full cycle test, check header-rail seams and carpet for water. Rocker panel rust check. Confirm manual vs. auto. Check for proper oil pressure at idle (2.4 Ecotec is tough but hates neglect). Verify no clunks from rear suspension bushings.
picks.bottomLine: Best price-per-fun ratio in the search — $7,500 for a clean Solstice roadster is hard to beat.

picks.whyGood: 44,000 miles on a 1994 GT is legitimately unusual — this car has barely been driven. The SN-95 5.0L V8 produces 215hp and sounds phenomenal through the factory exhaust. Private seller means no dealer markup. At $10,500 for these miles, it's priced for condition, not just age.
picks.whatToVerify: SN-95 top inspection: check the convertible top mount-to-rocker frame weld for rust (common failure point). Operate the top. Check rear window defroster. With only 44k miles, ask for service history — long periods of sitting can mean dried seals, old coolant, stale fuel issues. Test all electronics (window switches, A/C). Manual or auto — GT with T-5 manual is far more desirable.
picks.bottomLine: 44,000 miles on a V8 Mustang convertible at $10,500 is genuinely rare — inspect closely and buy if rust-free.

picks.whyGood: The E46 325Ci convertible is BMW's peak era: silky M54 inline-6, proper rear-wheel drive, beautiful proportions. At 60,200 miles and $10,000, this is well within reasonable range for the chassis. Great Deal badge + 'High Demand' flag from Cars.com. Close to Media at 16 miles. E46 convertibles hold up well mechanically — the top is durable when maintained.
picks.whatToVerify: E46 convertible top: power mechanism and hydraulic cylinders can fail — test full cycle. Check for rear-window seal leaks (water stains on rear seat). Oil leaks are common on M54 (valve cover gasket, VANOS seals). Check coolant level and reservoir for cracks. Ask for timing chain service history (guides can fail). Pull codes.
picks.bottomLine: 16 miles from Media, Great Deal badge, 60k miles — one of the better E46 Ci values in the region right now.

picks.whyGood: The Z3 2.8 (M52TU or M54, 189hp 6-cyl) is the sweet spot in the Z3 range — more power than the 1.9 or 2.3, without M Roadster premium pricing. At $7,940 from a private seller (no dealer fees), this is strong value for a classic BMW roadster at 75k miles. Private sellers often have more complete history than dealers.
picks.whatToVerify: Z3 convertible top: vinyl/canvas condition, rear plastic window for yellowing/cracks. Check for rust on trunk floor (drainage clogged). Oil leaks from M52/M54 are normal — look for fresh seal work. VANOS rattle on cold start. Cooling system: Z3 is prone to coolant leaks from expansion tank and hoses — inspect carefully. Ask for maintenance records.
picks.bottomLine: Best-value BMW roadster in the search — 6-cylinder Z3 at $7,940 private sale is worth a serious look.

picks.whyGood: The R59 MINI Roadster is a proper two-seat roadster (not the regular hatchback-based Convertible). The Cooper S trim adds a 181hp 1.6T engine and sportier suspension. At 48,374 miles with a Great Deal badge at $10,990, this is a relatively low-mileage modern car. MINI Roadsters are fun, well-built, and easier to maintain than German luxury alternatives.
picks.whatToVerify: Operate soft-top through full cycle. Check for top seam leaks at A-pillar. MINI N18 turbo (S trim) — check for coolant leaks from turbo coolant lines. Check oil for burning (some N18 engines consume oil — verify level). Check rear window zip condition on soft top. Timing chain on N18 has known issues — ask for inspection/replacement history.
picks.bottomLine: Low-mile, purpose-built roadster with turbo power and a Great Deal badge — strong pick at $10,990.

picks.whyGood: The B7 Audi A4 Cabriolet in quattro is a well-rounded drop-top: all-wheel drive, 200hp 2.0T FSI, proper back seats (useful), and Audi build quality. At 62,185 miles with a Great Deal badge from Selden Motors (reputable Willow Grove dealer), this is fairly priced. Quattro AWD is genuinely useful in PA weather.
picks.whatToVerify: The A4 Cabriolet has a power soft top — test full cycle and listen for hydraulic pump effort. Check for leaks at rear window seam. B7 2.0T FSI: check for carbon buildup on intake valves (direct injection), oil consumption, and timing chain tensioner (common issue on early FSI). Quattro front differential seal leaks. Check for torn CV boots.
picks.bottomLine: Quattro AWD convertible at 62k miles and Great Deal pricing — practical fun, not just fair-weather fun.

picks.whyGood: The 986 Boxster is one of the best-driving sports cars ever made at any price — mid-engine balance, precise steering, fantastic chassis. At $11,250 and 74,000 miles with a Good Deal badge, this is in the right zone. The 2001 uses the 2.7L flat-six (217hp). The key caveat — IMS bearing — is manageable if verified.
picks.whatToVerify: IMS BEARING IS THE MAKE-OR-BREAK: on 1997–2005 986/996 Porsches, the intermediate shaft bearing can fail catastrophically. Ask for documented IMS retrofit (LN Engineering kit) or IMS-bearing replacement — if not done, budget $800–1,500 for the fix and factor into price. Inspect for oil leaks from RMS (rear main seal) and valley leaks. Top cycle test. Check AC operation (evaporator is expensive on Boxster). Pull codes.
picks.bottomLine: Greatest sports car in this search on pure driving feel — buy only with documented IMS work or negotiate room for the fix.

picks.whyGood: 177,000 miles on a Miata is not the red flag it would be on most cars. The NC-gen (2006–2015) 2.0L MZR engine is legendary for durability — 200k+ miles is common with basic maintenance. The Grand Touring trim adds leather, Bose audio, and the power retractable hardtop (PRHT). At $11,450, you're buying proven reliability in the most purpose-built roadster ever made.
picks.whatToVerify: High-miles Miata PPI: check timing chain (NC uses a chain, not belt — low maintenance), look for oil leaks from cam seals and rear main, inspect clutch wear (NC clutch is strong but 177k miles means replacement may be due). PRHT mechanism: test full cycle, listen for motor strain. Check for rust on sill panels and rear wheel arches. This is not an early Miata — no timing belt to worry about.
picks.bottomLine: 177k miles on a Miata is fine — it's the most reliable convertible platform in this search, period.

picks.whyGood: The E46 330ci uses the 3.0L M54 inline-6 (228hp) vs. the 325i's 2.5L — meaningfully more power and better throttle response. Private seller, 88,600 miles, $10,500 — reasonable for an E46 Ci with the bigger engine. The M54 is one of the best BMW engines of the era: smooth, durable, and well-supported by the independent shop community.
picks.whatToVerify: Same E46 checks as the 325Ci above: power top hydraulics, rear window seal, cooling system (critical — E46 cooling components fail around 100k), oil leaks from VANOS and valve cover. Confirm M54 vs. M52 engine code (2004 should be M54). Check for subframe reinforcement (E46 sedans have known subframe cracks; convertible is less common but still inspect). Pull codes.
picks.bottomLine: The 3.0L E46 convertible at private-sale pricing — a better engine than the 325 at the same price point.

mustangs.introHtml: Ford Mustang convertibles in budget, ranked by trim (GT first) then mileage. The SN-95 GTs (1994–1995) are the highlight — both have under 60k miles at ~$10,500. The S197 and S550 options fill out the list for buyers who want something more modern.

picks.whyGood: 44,000 miles on an SN-95 GT convertible. The 5.0L V8 (215hp) sounds like a proper pony car should. Private seller = no dealer markup. These low-mile SN-95 GTs are getting harder to find as survivors.
picks.whatToVerify: Check convertible top mount-to-rocker welds for rust (SN-95 specific failure point). With 44k miles, ask about storage history — long sits cause dried seals, stale fuel, cracked hoses. Verify T-5 manual (much preferred over auto). Test all electronics. Inspect rear seat area for water staining from top seal.
picks.bottomLine: Best Mustang in the search by far — 44k-mile GT convertible at private-sale price is a genuine find.

picks.whyGood: Another low-mile SN-95 GT at essentially the same price. 55,630 miles on a 1995 5.0L V8. At this mileage the engine should be strong. GT Motors is a dealer, so some markup is baked in — room to negotiate.
picks.whatToVerify: Same SN-95 PPI: rocker/frame rust at convertible top mount, top cycle test, rear window condition (plastic or glass?). Third-gear synchro test on T-5 if manual. 5.0L: check for head gasket seep and timing chain noise.
picks.bottomLine: Comparable to #1 — same era, slightly more miles, dealer instead of private. Both are strong picks.

picks.whyGood: The S197 (2005–2014) V6 is the modern, refined choice — 305hp (2011+), decent fuel economy, no IRS clunk issues on the V6. Great Deal + 'High Demand' badge at 61k miles. 20 miles from Media. Trade-off: it's a V6, not a GT, so less theater — but more reliable and cheaper to insure.
picks.whatToVerify: S197 convertible PPI: check rear seat for water staining (top seal failure is common). Test top cycle. S197 T-3650 transmission: slow-shift to third to test synchro. Rear axle clunk on takeoff (check pinion bearing). Check for IRS (only on some trims/years — GT500 and some specials). Inspect door seals.
picks.bottomLine: Best modern Mustang convertible in the search — Great Deal badge, 61k miles, 20 miles away.

picks.whyGood: Closest Mustang to Media at just 9 miles (West Chester). 96,539 miles is manageable on the 3.7L V6 (305hp). $11,459 is reasonable. Fair Deal rating means some room to negotiate.
picks.whatToVerify: S197 top cycle, water staining check. At 96k miles inspect for any deferred maintenance. T-3650 third-gear synchro test. Exhaust for burning oil. Check rear axle clunk.
picks.bottomLine: Closest Mustang to home base — good option if proximity matters and you negotiate on the Fair Deal pricing.

picks.whyGood: Newest Mustang convertible in the search — 2018 S550 with EcoBoost 2.3T (310hp). Premium trim includes leather, larger infotainment, MagneRide suspension on some builds. The EcoBoost is genuinely quick (0-60 ~5.5s) and gets 30+ mpg highway. Trade-off: 141,767 miles and Fair Deal rating — not the best value.
picks.whatToVerify: S550 EcoBoost: check for cooling system health (intercooler hoses, coolant temp). Top cycle and seal inspection. 141k miles — inspect timing chain cover for seep. Check for tune/modifications (EcoBoost is popular for aftermarket work). Verify convertible top mechanism operates smoothly (S550 power top is generally reliable but check motor noise).
picks.bottomLine: Most modern option but 141k miles at a Fair Deal price — negotiate hard or keep shopping for a lower-mile S550.
